notes: Last confirmed close is Friday, May 15. US markets are closed over the weekend. A handful of sources reported a slightly lower intraday quote of $418.57; the official close of $422.24 was cross-checked across Yahoo Finance, MacroTrends, Stock Analysis and TradingView.

news.sentiment_summary: Sentiment toward Tesla flipped from euphoric to cautious in the last 48 hours. Coming off a 30%+ recovery rally from the April low, the stock gave back roughly 5% on Friday after the Trump–Xi summit ended without a deal on Full Self-Driving approval in China — the single biggest potential growth lever investors had been pricing in. Underneath the headline drop, the bullish robotaxi-expansion story is still very much alive (39 unsupervised vehicles now operating, with launches planned in another five US cities), but it is fighting against fresh regulatory worries: an escalated US FSD probe covering 3.2M vehicles, an unflattering Australian court ruling, and a Q1 delivery miss that left dealers with their largest inventory build ever. The mood is best described as 'taking profits on the easy money, waiting to see who blinks first.'

items.headline: Tesla falls 4.75% on Friday as Trump–Xi summit ends with no China FSD deal
items.summary: Tesla closed at $422.24 on May 15, down roughly $21 in a single session, after President Trump's high-profile trip to Beijing — which Elon Musk personally joined — produced no agreement on Chinese regulatory approval for Full Self-Driving. China FSD revenue had been pencilled into many bull cases as a needle-mover, so its absence forced a reset of expectations. Tesla ended Q1 with 1.3M global FSD subscribers, none of them in China.

items.headline: US auto safety regulator escalates probe of 3.2 million Tesla FSD vehicles
items.summary: NHTSA upgraded its investigation into Tesla's Full Self-Driving system, citing concerns that the software may fail to detect or warn drivers in poor visibility conditions. The probe now covers 3.2 million vehicles — a sweeping pool that includes most of Tesla's US fleet. An escalation does not automatically lead to a recall, but it raises the political ceiling on how aggressively Tesla can keep expanding its robotaxi geofences in the US.

items.headline: Tesla robotaxi fleet hits 39 vehicles; Dallas and Houston go live
items.summary: Tesla's unsupervised robotaxi fleet has more than quadrupled since the start of April, growing from 9 to 39 vehicles. Dallas (31 sq mi) and Houston (25 sq mi) zones are now operational alongside Austin and the Bay Area, taking total covered area to roughly 1,190 sq mi. Phoenix, Miami, Orlando, Tampa and Las Vegas are next on the rollout list. Morgan Stanley called the pace 'a material evolution' and modelled 1,000 vehicles by year-end.

items.headline: Tesla wins approval to test Full Self-Driving in Belgium's Flanders region
items.summary: Belgian transport authorities cleared Tesla to begin supervised FSD trials in the Flanders region — a meaningful European foothold given that Europe has been Tesla's slowest FSD market. The approval is narrow (testing only, not paid rides) but sets a regulatory precedent other EU regions may follow. Investors viewed this as a partial offset to the China disappointment.
items.source: Capital Street FX / Foreign Policy Journal

items.headline: Tesla raises Model Y prices in the US for the first time in two years
items.summary: Tesla quietly increased Model Y sticker prices across all US trims — a small bump, but symbolically important after two years of price cuts. Management is signalling that demand has stabilised enough to lean on price rather than discount, although bears point out that Q1 inventory swelled by more than 50,000 units, which makes any price hike a high-stakes bet on demand elasticity.

analysis_7d.ma_cross_status: Golden Cross still intact — 50-day above 200-day, long-term bullish alignment holds
analysis_7d.bollinger_position: Middle band — pulled back from the upper band after the Friday sell-off
analysis_7d.volume_trend: Heavy volume on Friday's drop — sellers were committed, not just noise
technical_signals: Price is above the 200-day average but just dropped below the 5-day and 50-day averages — a short-term yellow flag
RSI fell from above 70 (overbought) to around 60 in one session — the heat is coming out of the rally
MACD looks set to roll over after a Friday close below the signal line — momentum is shifting
Volume on Friday's down day was elevated — a conviction sell, not a low-energy drift
analysis_7d.summary: This week told two stories. From Monday through Thursday, Tesla looked unstoppable — the stock punched above $445 on robotaxi-expansion and Belgium FSD news. Then Friday happened: the Trump–Xi summit ended without China FSD approval and the stock dropped almost 5% on heavy volume, wiping out most of the week's gains. The damage is short-term — Tesla is still above its 200-day moving average and the longer-term Golden Cross is intact — but the easy momentum trade is over for now.

analysis_30d.trend: strong recovery uptrend, with a sharp Friday pullback

key_levels.support_reason: Buyers stepped in firmly around the $398–$400 zone twice in early May — it has become the floor for this rally
key_levels.resistance_reason: Sellers appeared aggressively around $445 mid-week, and Friday's reversal off that level confirms it as the short-term ceiling

analysis_30d.rsi_label: Neutral — cooled off from overbought, plenty of room either way
analysis_30d.macd_signal: Weakening bullish — losing momentum after the rally peak
notable_events: Apr 22: Q1 2026 earnings — EPS beat ($0.41 vs $0.36), revenue beat ($22.39B), but Q1 deliveries missed at 358K and inventory swelled by 50K+ units
Late Apr / Early May: ~32% rally from the $336 April low on robotaxi expansion to Dallas and Houston
May 11–12: Robotaxi fleet reaches 39 vehicles; Morgan Stanley calls expansion 'a material evolution'
May 14: NHTSA escalates Full Self-Driving probe to cover 3.2M vehicles
May 15: Trump–Xi summit ends without China FSD approval — stock drops 4.75% on heavy volume
analysis_30d.summary: Step back from the noise and the 30-day picture is still a recovery story. Tesla bottomed near $336 in early April and grinded all the way back to $445 mid-May — a ~32% bounce — on robotaxi expansion, the Q1 earnings beat, and FSD approval in Europe. Friday's 5% drop on the failed China deal took some shine off but only partially. The stock now sits between its 50-day average ($428) and 200-day average ($418) — a coiled, indecisive position that usually resolves with a sharp move one way or the other.

outlook.recent_rating_change: Morgan Stanley reiterated Overweight on May 14, calling the robotaxi expansion 'a material evolution' and raising its fleet target to 1,000 vehicles by end-2026

catalysts.detail: Q2 is the first quarter where robotaxi revenue should be a visible line item, and analysts want to see auto gross margin recover above 17%. A clean beat plus margin recovery would re-rate the stock.

catalysts.event: Next round of robotaxi city launches (Phoenix, Miami, Orlando)
catalysts.detail: Each new city adds geofenced revenue and validates the playbook. A safety incident in any city would do the opposite damage.

catalysts.timing: Open-ended, could escalate at any time
catalysts.detail: A formal recall recommendation covering 3.2M vehicles would be expensive and would force the robotaxi rollout to slow — the single biggest US-side overhang on the stock.
If Beijing eventually approves FSD, it unlocks the world's largest car market for Tesla's highest-margin software product. Failure of the Trump–Xi summit means this is on ice for now.
Cybercab is the purpose-built robotaxi vehicle. A real volume ramp would shift the story from 'Tesla retrofits cars' to 'Tesla manufactures a fleet' — a different valuation conversation.

macro_factors.detail: No FSD approval and ongoing tariff risk both squeeze Tesla's China growth and margins simultaneously.

macro_factors.factor: US interest rates and EV-loan financing costs
macro_factors.detail: Rates remain elevated, which weighs on big-ticket vehicle financing and on the present value of Tesla's future cash flows — but the Fed has signalled patience, so no immediate shock.

macro_factors.factor: Global EV competition (BYD, Chinese OEMs, legacy automakers)
macro_factors.detail: BYD's price advantage in Asia and Europe continues to compress Tesla's market share, and Tesla's recent Model Y price hike is a high-stakes test of demand elasticity.

outlook.summary: The next 3–6 months will be a tug-of-war between robotaxi execution (the bull case) and regulatory drag (the bear case). Best case: Tesla launches three more robotaxi cities cleanly, Q2 earnings deliver a margin beat, and the NHTSA probe fades quietly — that path takes the stock back to the $500 zone and re-ignites the 'platform business' narrative. Base case: Tesla muddles through, robotaxi expansion continues but Q2 margins disappoint and the stock chops in a $380–$460 range. Worst case: a robotaxi safety incident or formal NHTSA recall combined with continued China silence would crush the multiple and revisit the April lows. Position size for both outcomes.

recommendation.reasoning: The technical picture is genuinely split. On the bullish side, Tesla is still above its 200-day moving average, the Golden Cross is intact, the 30-day trend is firmly up, the robotaxi rollout is accelerating with Morgan Stanley raising its targets, and analyst average price targets are close to today's price. On the bearish side, Friday's 4.75% drop on heavy volume just pushed the stock below the 50-day and 5-day averages, MACD is rolling over, the NHTSA probe was just escalated, China FSD approval failed, and Q1 deliveries missed with a huge inventory build. With five clear signals on each side, the honest answer is to hold what you own, wait for the next directional confirmation (either a successful defence of $400 support or a clean break above $445 resistance), and let Q2 earnings on July 22 do the talking. Anything bolder is a guess.
recommendation.key_risk: An NHTSA recall recommendation on Full Self-Driving — covering 3.2M vehicles — would simultaneously hit margins, slow the robotaxi rollout, and damage the AI narrative that is doing most of the heavy lifting in the bull case. That is the single biggest thing that could break this call.
